title: How Your Eyes Can Detect a Single Photon
summary: A rod photoreceptor can respond to a single photon, but conscious detection usually requires the retina and brain to separate that tiny signal from biological noise.

lesson.learn: Human vision reaches astonishing sensitivity in darkness. At the level of biology, a rod photoreceptor can respond when a single photon changes one molecule of rhodopsin. That molecular event triggers a phototransduction cascade, amplifying a tiny physical hit into an electrical change the retina can process. But detecting a photon in a rod is not the same as consciously seeing one. The visual system is noisy: molecules can activate spontaneously, neurons have baseline activity, and light scatters before reaching the retina. To avoid hallucinating flashes from random noise, the brain usually requires enough coordinated evidence across retinal circuits before perception crosses awareness. Classic experiments in the 1940s found that people could report extremely faint flashes involving only a small number of photons reaching the retina. Modern experiments have pushed the question further, showing that humans can perform above chance when single-photon events are carefully controlled. The miracle is not that the eye is perfect; it is that it is sensitive while still filtering uncertainty.
lesson.simpler: Rod cells can react to single photons, but seeing one consciously is harder because the retina and brain must decide whether the signal is real or just noise.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Single-photon vision is possible because rod cells amplify tiny molecular events, while retinal and brain circuits filter those events before they become conscious perception.
lesson.deeperDive: Rods are specialized for low-light vision. Each rod contains stacks of membrane discs packed with rhodopsin, a light-sensitive protein bound to retinal. When a photon is absorbed, retinal changes shape, activating rhodopsin and starting a biochemical cascade that reduces cyclic GMP, closes ion channels, and changes the cell’s electrical output. This amplification makes a single photon biologically meaningful. The retina then pools and compares rod signals through bipolar, horizontal, amacrine, and ganglion cells. Pooling improves sensitivity but reduces detail, which is why dim vision is grainy and color-poor. Perception depends on signal detection: the nervous system must balance sensitivity against false alarms from thermal noise and spontaneous molecular activity. That tradeoff explains why the eye can approach quantum limits without making every random fluctuation visible.
lesson.funFact: In deep darkness, your eyes are sensitive enough to operate near the edge of physics.

references.title: Tinsley et al., Direct detection of a single photon by humans

people: Selig Hecht - coauthor of classic threshold vision experiments
Simon Tinsley - coauthor of modern single-photon vision research
places: Retina - light-sensitive neural tissue at the back of the eye
Rod outer segment - rhodopsin-rich structure where photon detection begins
years: 1942 - classic experiments estimated the faintest light humans can see
2016 - modern work tested human responses to single-photon stimuli
concepts: Rod photoreceptor - retinal cell specialized for dim light
Rhodopsin - light-sensitive protein in rod cells
Phototransduction - biochemical conversion of light into neural signal
